description: Apollo 17 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 2: Lunar Orbit Insertion

tokens: At 088:43:21, the Apollo 17 stack passed around the western limb of the Moon with respect to the ground station on Earth that was handling their communication.
It is heading towards its closest approach to the far side on its current trajectory which will be just 52.5 nautical miles (97.2 km) altitude.
At about this point, the SPS engine will be ignited to reduce their speed and rob them of the momentum that would take the spacecraft away from the Moon again.
This will place them in an initial orbit measuring 52 by 170 nautical miles [96 by 315 km].
Note that there is no recording of the crew conversations covering LOI which is unusual for the Apollo missions.
As detailed at the end of this nearside pass, a step was missed prior to LOI when setting up the bit rate used to record engine data.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 089:15:30 -This is Apollo Control at 89 hours, 15 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Some 55 seconds until Apollo 17 comes from behind the Moon, on the start of the first lunar orbit.
We're awaiting appearance of the spacecraft and word that the burn went successful, which apparently it has because the spacecraft without a burn would have appeared some 8 minutes ago.
The ignition time was 88:54:21 in Ground Elapsed Time.
Should be getting lock-on fairly soon, zero.
That was the theoretical acquisition time.
Of course, it does take a few seconds for the ground station to lock on to the signal from the spacecraft.
We're awaiting word from the communications officer that we have indeed gotten a signal and telemetry from the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 089:20:05 -This is Apollo Control.

Here in the Control Center we are hearing the crew aboard the spacecraft of Apollo 17 very clearly, however the ground has not be able to fully lock up on the spacecraft with the so-called uplink, on the voice uplink.
And apparently it has to do with the 85-foot [26-metre] antenna at Goldstone.
We're continuing to stand by until the 2-way communications are complete.
An hour and 20 minutes before Loss of Signal as we go across the face of the Moon, the front face of the Moon on the first lunar orbit.
At 89:20 and standing by, this is Apollo Control.

The CMC holds us in a 170.3 by 52.5 [nautical-mile orbit, 315.4 by 97.2 km].

tokens: Houston, this is Jack.
The PUGS was erratic.
It's the only thing I noticed that was off-nominal.

It - it bounced ground a lot, in bouncing arou - initially for about a minute - around - oh, 1.8 decrease, then it gradually started to diverge from that to a more decrease, down to about 2.5 or make that 3 decrease.

And I went to Decrease on the switch; and about the time of crossover, I had it at - oh, about 1 decrease and it crossed over and stabilized at zero, and I went to Normal and it stayed there the rest of the burn - until just near the end, it started to go decrease again.

tokens: And, Houston - Ron here.
Something a little bit of a surprise to me.
When I turned on bank A, the chamber pressure came up to 87 percent, and stayed there essentially.
Five seconds later, I put on bank B, the chamber pressure rose to 90 - about 91 - somewhere in that area.
And then, throughout the burn, chamber pressure increased, which you'll see on the strip chart.
But I was sure surprised at only 87 percent when we started it.
It looked like the velocity gained versus time was correct throughout the burn, though.
The maximum the chamber pressure ever got to was about 97 percent, and that was toward the end of the burn.

tokens: At the time, Proclus was the subject of some debate among lunar geologists as they tried to explain its ray system.
It was clear that crater rays were formed from light-coloured ejecta thrown out from the point of impact.
But in the case of Proclus, about 120° of angle around the crater was almost completely excluded from its system of rays.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One explanation that was linked to the very polygonal outline of the crater was that some sort of subsurface faulting had led to part of the ejecta being shadowed.
Now we know that the distinctive exclusion is due to Proclus having been formed from a very low-angle impact by a projectile travelling approximately southwest to northeast.
Such distinctive asymmetric ray systems are actually quite common and a fine example is the most magnificent ray system on the Moon, that of the crater Tycho.
It's impactor came in from the west, creating an exclusion zone to the west where the rays are more subdued.
This is bounded by two prominent rays.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Infamous Proclus.

transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: As viewed from Earth, the Moon is only a few days past new and it is therefore presenting a crescent phase.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If the Moon appears as a crescent to Earth, then Earth appears gibbous, nearly full, to the Apollo 17 crew.
It is also four times the Moon's apparent diameter and covered with highly reflective cloud.
As they pass over the terminator, Earth's light is enough to gently illuminate the Moon's night-time surface, allowing Ron to distinguish the dark horizon that they are approaching.
The fact that most of the near side is in darkness means that they will only pass sunlit terrain for the minority of their nearside pass.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Boy, you sure can.

I remember Bill Anders talking about the appearance of a sea swell within the mare itself and that's certainly clearly shown right at the terminator.
Plus you start to see the shadows from all the very small craters that otherwise don't show up as much more that just little depressions, if that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
We need Accept so we can give you a landing site REFSMMAT.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, you have it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This new REFSMMAT represents an orientation that the crew will use for the landing.
It is the reference orientation for the upcoming Descent Orbit Insertion burn, the powered descent and landing, and the CSM's activities up to when Ron carried out a plane change burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The landing site REFSMMAT is based around the intended moment of landing and it imagines the orientation in space of the landing site at that moment.
The IMU X-axis will be parallel to a line that runs from the Moon's centre through the landing site.
The Z-axis will be parallel to the landing site's horizontal plane but run in a direction parallel to the CSM's orbital plane.

The IMUs in the the CSM and the LM will be aligned per this REFSMMAT and the upshot is that if the LM lands in a nominal fashion (feet level and with no yaw), then its FDAI should display 0° in all axes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.

Before we really got concentrating on the burn, and I had an opportunity to - we all had an opportunity to look at Korolev, at a very low grazing Sun, one of the striking things was the - to me was the extreme absence of relief, the very smooth surface that existed in Korolev, independent, of course, of the craters that are penetrating that surface.

And it looked like there was a ring in the floor - outsi - next to the wall, about maybe sixth of a crater radius, that was somewhat brighter at the low grazing Sun, suggesting it may have had a different slope.
And I believe I'm correct in saying that the inner floor may be slightly raised.
We'll try to make other observations this next time around on that one, but there was just a very smooth floor.
The light plains material in that crater is very smooth.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; understand.
One quick word from FAO's...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And, of course, that's independent...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

We'd rather you take the optional photographs on either OO or PP and reserve KK for the scheduled photographs.
And, as it stands now, we've got to reserve 80 frames on either OO or PP for scheduled photography.
Over.

But you did lose the high bit rate.
is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's affirmative.
We just had low bit rate during the burn.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
I may have jumped the gun on you there a little bit.
But I thought we were supposed to do that just before we went AOS - LOS.
I think I did it about a minute before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
It was almost identi - simultaneous with our sending the command.
You've got about 4 minutes to start looking at Copernicus coming here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
We're just - Sun's just setting.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I have a visual on Copernicus.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And by the way, the S-IVB crunched in on time, and it's been ringing the ALSEP seismometers like mad for some time now.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: VOX, and that's VOX.
How do you read the LMP, Gordy?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Loud and clear, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Thank you.
Okay, good.
All right, I've got a visual on Eratosthenes and Copernicus.
The - they're obviously different-age craters in this light.
You can see the ray patterns in Copernicus moderately well.
You can even tell that they do cross Eratosthenes.
Stadius shows up as a very clear dark area to the southwest of Eratosthenes.
Now, one of the things that we mapped on the southeast and south rim of Copernicus were dark albedo areas within the ejecta.
And those are apparent here, very clearly.
And also, within - on the upper portion of the rim and on the benches, in that quadrant.
The walls - there are the dark spots - lower albedo material that we mapped.
They are - form linear patterns along the benches, apparently.
Although the bench - the topography is not too clear.
But the dark spots are in arcuate linear arrangement parallel to the rim.
And they appear to have - be elongate along radius - along the radius of the crater.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: They are in the high part of their orbit and are reaching the western side of the Moon as seen from Earth.
This landscape is only illuminated by Earthlight which is nearly overhead.
There is little sense of topography but the inherent reflectance of the Moon's surface, its albedo, is easily apparent.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jack Schmitt is unlike all the other crewmen who visited the Moon during Apollo.
Whereas they were all professional, often military pilots who instinctively kept off the radio except when there was an operational reason, Jack is a scientist by training.
He learned to fly fast jets as part of his astronaut training but his instinct is to describe.
Now in lunar orbit, this lunar geologist has begun a campaign of observation and description that will continue throughout his time at the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is in the southeast - southeast quadrant I'm referring to.
Copernicus H is also very obvious as a dark-rimmed crater, relative to the albedo of the ejecta blanket.
And the northwest quadrant, which we mapped as a smooth floor material and somewhat darker albedo, is just as apparent here, although all the contrasts, of course, are less.
[Pause.] The main thing that you can pick out in Earthshine are albedo distinctions.
Now - now we - we - I remember that we also, at one time - and I can't remember whether it made the final map or not - mapped an arcuate fault structure, based on the change in albedo.
And this was on the southwest quadrant of Copernicus.
And that crossed the southern rim, more or less north-south, went down and curved across the floor and up the southwest rim, and that is a very clear arcuate pattern in this lighting.
Darker albedo than the rest of the crater.
And along the arc, there appear to be a couple or three even darker spots - much as we thought we saw in some of the early photography.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: There is a general streaking - radial streaking - within that arcuate dark area that radiates radially from the crater, and if I had to project, the dark area would - north - whose northeast terminus is the arc in the crater, I'd project it off to southeast about crater diameter and a half, maybe.

[Pause.] Now there's - I'm getting - essentially directly overhead, and again the dark spots within the crater wall are still apparent, and the streaming or the radial elongation of these spots is clear.

Now, on the lower wall - that's below the first bench - I can see four of these dark areas, and on the next wall above that, above the first bench, there are two - two obvious ones and a couple that are somewhat more subtle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: In the middle of the...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...Can you see anything of the - that stuff we were wondering about in the central peaks?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: No, Gordy, I can't.
The - there's very little indication of relief in Earthshine and particularly at this high Sun ang - high Earth angle, pardon the expression.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The central peaks, they do stand out, though, as a much lighter albedo area within the crater.
It looks to me like the best thing you can do in Earthshine is work with albedos.
Knowing the general topography from the - from the pre - earlier photography.
We're coming up on Kepler now.
Copernicus is out of my view in window 5 - Maybe Ron sees it.
Do you, Ron?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, it's out of my view now, too.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, we, we...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Can you see Reinhold over there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Reinhold, I was not conscious - Yes, I can see Reinhold.
Reinhold is one of the craters just like Copernicus that may have contributed ejecta into the Apollo 12 soil.
Do you see anything...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Copernicus is a little under 400 kilometres north-northwest of the Apollo 12 landing site but its extensive and now fading ray system can clearly be seen to overlie where Pete Conrad and Al Bean had sampled the soil on that mission.
Copernicus is used as an exemplar for one of the geological age classifications - Copernican.
Reinhold is about half of the diameter of Copernicus and is sited approximately midway between Copernicus and Apollo 12.
Although its age means that its ray system has completely faded, the ejecta from its formation is also likely to be part of the soil at the 12 site.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As Jack is about to relate, the lack of rays coming from Reinhold places the formation of the crater to an older lunar era, the Eratosthenian.
This period of time is named after the crater Eratosthenes which, like Reinhold, also lacks rays yet is clearly superimposed on the maria.

Since the belief is that rays fade with age so craters without them are older than about 1 to 1.5 billion years, but they are clearly not older than the maria with are dated to about 3 to 3.8 billion years old.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: An S - an S-IVB - 16's S-IVB hit out there to the south, I think.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Well, I don't...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, I don't think you'd see it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: As occurred with the Apollo 17 S-IVB, the third stages of the later launch vehicles were sent to impact the Moon in order to provide a calibrated seismic signal to the seismometers that had already been emplaced by previous crews.
On impact, each rocket stage created an irregular 30-metre crater.
The S-band radios in the S-IVBs allowed them to be tracked to the surface which helped locate exactly where they had impacted, except in the case of Apollo 16.
Its radio failed 27 hours into the mission and engineers were therefore uncertain where it met its end.
Its crater was not located until 2015 in imagery from Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter.
It position was determined as 1°, 55 minutes, 15.6 seconds north; 335°, 22 minutes, 37.2 seconds east.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't see anything that would indicate that.
Reinhold is an Eratosthenian Age crater, as you may recall.
It's crossed, very obviously even in this light, by the Copernican rays, which is prime - the main way we determine the age, other than it doesn't have any rays itself.
Although, on the good photography we now have, we know it has secondary crater patterns around it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're not looking at the same crater I am then.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Reinhold is right down here, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Where are you looking [garble]?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, it's south of Copernicus.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I'm looking up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: South and a little west.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't have the same view you have.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I can see Lansberg now.
And I'm afraid I can't shed any light, pardon the expression, on the old question we've had about the age of Lansberg relative to the mare.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Kepler ray pattern is very striking in this light, An anastomoting - mosing series of - of bands which only average being radial.
In most cases, they're a little off radial, but by joining together, they give you a general radial pattern.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I just wanted to see a...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Out the window 4, I have an excellent view of Kepler.

Once again, albedo differences, such as the - are very clear - such as the distinction between the wall - light - or brighter wall materials and the rim which, in itself, is brighter than the surrounding mare.
We can look right down some of the rays, and the rays are not completely linear.
I'm looking now to the northwest.
Gene, you can see Aristarchus way up there in the northwest.
See it?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah.
It's coming into [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You ought to have a good view of Aristarchus.
Very bright, up in the northwest part of our field of view.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The inside of that crater almost looks as if it's backlit.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Aristarchus is a relatively young (450 million years) crater that is distinguished by having the highest reflectivity on the Moon.
It is well north of their track but is visible thanks to the high apolune of their initial orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's right.
Well, it should be [chuckle].
Strangely enough.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And - but I want - these rays when contrast to the - maybe the feeling one would have that they are - once they get started, they form a linear pattern across the surface.
They don't seem to have done that.
As a matter of fact, they're quite strikingly curved along their pattern.
They'll break from the general radius they're out on, curve away and then curve back.
Some - all - let's see, there are one - one, two, three, four - four rays that go from out - to the northwest of Kepler, all of which show that nonlinear characteristic.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, they're all radial, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They are radial, in general.
But when you look at them in detail, only portions of them are radial.
And a single ray, which you can follow continuously, will bend and then curve back.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The - it's unique.
We've got rays from - I guess those must be Copernicus over here - those long ones out to the right, which you might not be able to see.
Kepler and Reiner are all - all intermingling up here.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Yeah, this is the area now we're going over where the gamma-ray work on 15 indicated a relatively high radioactivity.
And - not yet, it's...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Houston, America here.
Do you see the torquing angles?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I was going to look for the Hortensius domes near Copernicus, but got sidetracked with Kepler and missed them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, [garble] torquing angles, Ron.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ron is carrying out two P52 platform realignments, the 15th and 16th of the mission.
The first is to check the amount of drift since the previous realignment and the second will rotate the platform around according to the new landing site REFMMAT that was recently uploaded.
For the first realignment, Ron used stars 11 (Aldebaran, Alpha Tauri) and 16 (Procyon, Alpha Canis Minoris).
The angles by which the platform had drifted since the last P52 were -0.045° in X, -0.039° in Y and -0.020° in Z.
Ron's sightings were accurate to 0.01°, a good result, determined by comparing the measured angle between the two stars and the actual angle that the computer knows should be between them.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They are not obvious, though, in looking in the general direction...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I'll torque it to 8:45.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we're getting out into Procellarum - Oceanus Procellarum proper now and, before long, should pick up the medial ridge that makes that mare so distinctive from the other mare.
Actually, the ridge, it stretches something like 2,000 kilometers north to south, if you want to tie a vari - a number of things together.
And, probably is the lun - the Moon's closest analogy to an oceanic ridge, which has - which have been in the news over the last decade.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Once again, the topography of the Procellarum mare is not clear in this light.
It's just not quite enough light to give you a strong, distinct shadow, at least not yet at this Earth angle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
And as you get the camera set up for the orbital science pictures of Galois would want to change and use magazine OO instead of KK.
That's listed in the Flight Plan at 90:20.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we'll - we'll do that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay I'm - out of the window 4, I'm able to see some of the Reiner Gamma materials, and it's - awful hard to say more than just the fact there is a very clear light-colored pattern off to the north - of our position at any rate.
I think all I can say is that I've seen it.
I can't give you much information on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We can see the area where Marius Hills should be, although it's not an obvious topographic feature in this light.
Still see Aristarchus off up there shining like a star, if the Moon could have stars.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Houston.
I'm going to torque those cos - coarse align errors out at 1, 2.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Roger, Ron.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You squared away?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's good to be able to see some stars out of the telescope.
It's the first time I've been able to see any.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Gordy, we have a - a very clear zero phase point for the Earthlight.
It's certainly not a strong contrast for the Sun, but it's out there.
And within it, again, the fresh craters tend to brighten - quite a bit more than the surrounding mare.
Still looking at Oceanus Procellarum.

And now, out window 3, up to the northwest, Grimaldi is starting to show up - a very obvious dark area within the highlands of that part of the Moon - and one of the darkest mare regions that we have seen on the - on the Moon.
It's comparable, at least in the photographs, to that of Tsiolkovsky.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: When observing the Moon from Earth, it is noticeable that the lunar disc brightens substantially around the full phase, more than would be expected purely from the greater area of illumination.
Observers who are near the lunar surface see the same effect but localised, and find that the area surface around their shadow is similarly very bright.
This so-called zero phase effect is due partly to the loss of shadowing but a major contributor is a quantum mechanics effect known as coherent backscattering.

When light is scattered through a medium where the particles are similar in size to its wavelength, there is a reinforcement that occurs in the opposite direction of the incoming beam due to interference.
Jack is seeing the same zero phase effect for Earthlight impinging on the lunar surface.
Essentially, he is seeing a bright diffuse spot that is directly opposite Earth from the spacecraft traverse the landscape as they orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Normally, of course, we think of the dark mare as being the younger basalt flows that - on the Moon, but in our case, of course, young means something on the order of 3 billion years or older.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: For our interp - If we can extrapolate from the samples returned by other missions.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Amazing how far over - now the highlands to the west of Procellarum are - still are bright, and the contrast between fresh craters and the normal highland are very - are very obvious still in Earthlight, particularly along the zero phase point with respect to the Earth.
Reiner Gamma now is - is coming a little bit closer to our orbit track.
In the horseshoe - in the - larger and more western end of it; the dark horseshoe is quite clear in this light.
It's a west - or northwest-pointing horseshoe, as is the complete trend of that strange feature.
I think Ron is going to have an excellent chance to study these light-colored swirls within the mare and other parts of the Moon.
We had some good views of them and Mare Marginis and to the east of Crisium - Mare Crisium, and he should - if there's anything to be seen, he should be able to see it for - during the next few days.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okeydoke.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Say, Gordo, something I just noticed here in working with the GDC what have you.
I - looked at the PC gauge, and in the PZ - PC position, there is a continuous bias on it now of about, oh, 7 percent, and if I switch to ALPHA, it goes to zero.
We never saw that bias before this last burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, Gene.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: In the centre of panel 1 of the Main Display console is a meter that either displays the angle of attack of the Saturn V as it rises through the atmosphere (known as alpha), or it shows the pressure in the SPS engine's combustion chamber, PC.
Which parameter it displays is selected by a switch at the bottom of the panel.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Gordy, I'm looking right up the western edge of the Procellarum mare where it contacts the - the high - western highlands of the Moon, and we're just about to fly a little bit south of Grimaldi.
That edge is very irregular.
There is no obvious indication that it - there are large basins that have been flooded by mare that have formed that edge, but, again, the topographic distinctions possible in this light are small.
Now I'm starting to see that there are shadows in the craters.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: That's the small craters.
[Pause.] There, in the Mare Procellarum closest to Grimaldi, there are two arcuate rilles.
Looks like they're probably V-shaped in their cross section.
I'm sure we've seen those on the photographs much better than I can see them here.
Those - the rille patterns, though, do seem to project over into the highlands.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The arcuate rilles that are often seen curving around the margins of the major maria are believed to be due to the weight of the basalt that form the maria sinking a little.
This causes tension in the surrounding crust which is usually betrayed as a valley feature known as a graben where the crust breaks along two parallel faults and the land between sinks.
Jack's two graben can be seen running across the Crater Hevelius to the north which indicates that they are younger than the crater itself.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: To the north of that - to the north of that bay of mare.
Just interrupt.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, I just saw a flash on the lunar surface!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, yeah?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It was just out there north of Grimaldi.
Just north of Grimaldi.
You might see if you got anything on your seismometers, although a small impact probably would give a fair amount of visible light.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
We'll check.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It was a bright little flash right out there near that crater.
See the crater right at the edge of Grimaldi.
Then there is another one north of it.
Fairly sharp one north of it is where there was just a pin prick of light.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How about putting an X on the map where you saw it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I keep looking occasionally for...
[listens to Fullerton] yeah, we will.
I - I was planning on looking for those kind of things.
Starting to see the edge of Orientale, Gordy.
Way off to the west.
[Pause.] Hey, just yell, Gene, anytime you...
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gordy, to the north of Grimaldi there's a large basin that is about the same size but only incompletely filled with mare in its northeastern quadrant.
The rest of it looks like a fairly irregular and hummocky floor material of some kind.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Jack appears to be talking about Riccioli which matches his description except for being northwest of Grimaldi rather than north.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: But it's almost the same size as Grimaldi.
It even looks like it is a little bit deeper, but, of course, Grimaldi has considerable fill.
Grimaldi on its eastern edge has some of the graben, or its rim, if you will, is cut by three or four anastomosing grabens that make it look like some of the bench areas in the larger basins.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, the first ring of Orientale is showing up.
The inner - the next ring in is extremely obvious; got some very bright east - east-facing slopes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll turn off the intercom.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It has some very bright east-facing slopes, and you can see the bands of mare that are filling the - both the first bench area and the inner bench.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Any time you need us, Gordy, just interrupt me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; we'll do that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Now, as I look north along the first bench, that's the first bench from the outside, one inside the Cordillerian ring.
I better check that; I may have my names mixed up.
Got Orientale, there - handy?
[garble] see.
Should be on there.
Yeah, this is what I need.
What's the name - they got a name on that ridge, there?
That's Rook Mountains.
Yeah, the Cordillera.
Yeah, that's right.
Looking just west of the Cordillera on the first bench, as I look north, in this light, which is casting some shadows now, Gordy, over in here, it looks extremely smooth.
Now this is not mare; it's lighter albedo, lighter reflectivity than the mare, and, although there are patches of mare in the lower areas in it.
But looking along that plain, in fact, a lar - a long linear plain, it - it looks quite smooth with only some very broad undulations that appear to be roughly radial to Orientale itself.
The more - closer we get to it, the more I see minor relief showing up.
I start to see the shadows, I guess.
And that relief seems to bring out a hummocky texture in - in addition to the craters that you would expect to see there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: When looking at the Moon from Earth with the naked eye, the most obvious features are the dark patches.
These are the 'mare' which is Latin for sea.
Rather than water, these are great plains of basalt and they are often circular in outline.
We now understand that these basalt 'seas' were formed around 3 billion years ago and that they filled pre-existing basins that had been excavated by giant impacts.
Until the space age revealed the far side, no one had seen what an unfilled basin would look like.

Observers had noticed a range of mountains right at the Moon's western limb which only came into view at a favourable libration (the swaying from side to side that lets us see slightly more than half of the lunar surface from Earth).
These mountains seemed to form an arc and a small dark patch can occasionally be seen.
It was first named in 1906 by astronomer Julius Franz and the first clear image of the whole structure that showed it to be a multi-ring impact basin came from a Lunar Orbiter probe in the mid 1960s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I won't try to give you a trend on the hummocks because I think the shadows are biasing my view; they do appear to be north-south trending, but I think that is because of the shadow patterns.
This is a spectacular sight, you guys; you ought to take a look at Orientale.
One of the largest fresh basins on the Moon.
It still is probably 4 billion years old, or 3.8 at any rate, if our dating criteria are any - any good.
It has the outer Cordillerian ring and the inner ring called the Rook Mountains - very, very nicely shown.
There are massifs on that inner ring, the Rook Mountain ring; there are massif complexes much like what we'll be studying at Taurus-Littrow.
There are low areas, non-mare areas, that are comparable to some - to the Taurus - the valley that we'll be landing in.
And, all in all, I think we'll find that our ring in the Taurus-Littrow area around Serenitatis is comparable in many regards to this Rook Mountain ring around Orientale.
You want - did you interrupt, Gordy?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No.
I didn't say anything.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Mare Serenitatis is roughly circular but the form of the original basin is highly degraded because the nearby large impacts that formed the Imbrium and Crisium basins have obliterated much of its structure.
The structure of the Orientale basin is much clearer and it may be one of the youngest large multi-ring basins on the Moon even though it dates back nearly 4 billion years.
Since Mare Orientale is on the Moon's limb as seen from Earth, then it follows that Jack is about to lose any lighting on the landscape.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay; I thought I heard your key.
Okay; in the inner portion of Orientale, as we approach a terminator, the lighting is still excellent.
Matter of fact, it appears brighter than what we were looking at over at Copernicus.
Now, part of that may be we're seeing much sharper relief since the slope - Earth-facing slopes are nicely lit, and the backfacing slopes, of course, are in shadow.
The first portion of the bench inside the Rook Mountains is partially filled by mare.
Now, the higher land in there is very smooth - in a gross sense, is a very smooth hummocky terrain, cut by roughly circumferential grabens.
The trend of the hummocks themselves are not radial; they're more - well, they're about a 45-degree angle to the radius.
They, in detail, have a much finer hackly texture, much like we've been able to see on photographs before.
And, in general, you get the impression that, in several areas here, that that hackly textured surface is draped over material that resembles the massifs of the Rook Mountains themselves.
There's one area just to the north now of our track where there's a large, roughly equidimensional mountain mass, with a few projections of massif-like peaks through this hackly textured surface.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Jack's adjective 'hackly' refers to something that appears to have sharp, jagged surfaces.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There are also some radial grabens; I just now picked one up, which we've also seen on the Orientale photographs taken by Lunar Orbiter.
It's amazing how fresh appearing this basin looks, considering its great age.
But it's - it probably is not - had any more violent a history than Imbrium.
Now, we're getting up, just about to go over a delta-rim crater that's out in the basin.
I don't remember the name of it offhand [Kopff].
But it's - be familiar to some of the geologists who have mapped this basin.
It does not appear to have a strong impact ejecta blanket around it.
It's filled with mare, and it's quite sharply in contrast to a crater of comparable size to the northwest.
See that one, Ron; I don't know whether you can get it.
I get a good view out of 5 now of that one.

And, once again, it looks as if this hackly-textured material that forms the higher hills in the inner bench - has a draped appearance over the, over preexisting terrain, and, in fact, along the ridges of the hummocks, you - we now can pick up little rilles that roughly parallel the hummocks, although not - not - not consistently.
They do cross down into the valleys.
But it has appearance that there may have been a tensional relief along the crest of each of the hummocks, or many of the hummocks.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It appears that Jack is discussing the crater Kopff and is comparing it to the much more conventional central peak crater to its northwest, Maunder.

Further to what Jack is about to explain, a delta-rim crater is one where the form of the crater has been largely lost due to lava that has surrounded its raised rim and also risen up through fissures within the crater to fill its bowl.
All that's left is the circular rim sticking up whose cross-section is reminiscent of the Greek letter for Delta.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Delta-rim crater just as has been, I think, discussed in the literature, has just that, delta rim with no obvious ejecta blanket around it, compared to other larger craters within the basin.
We're directly over that crater right now.
It's filled with mare, very smooth mare.
Matter of fact, within that fill, I can see no - no craters.
[Pause.] Getting very close to the Earth terminator, but you see good texture in the ejecta blanket of the large crater [Maunder] in the north part of the inner basin of Orientale.

The radial ridge and valley patterns are very clear; the concentric coarse hummocks near the rim are apparent; and you can even see the second - patterns of secondaries, the larger secondaries, extending out away - radially out away from that crater.
The south - the mare fill in the south floor of Copernicus, I mean of Orientale, is very smooth but does have the sea-swell texture that we saw over in Tranquillitatis.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Better let him say something before AOS.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I ain't got nothing to say.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Houston, I guess we're getting close to AOS.
We're getting close to AOS.
Do you have some words for us?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We show about 9 minutes to LOS.
We'd like to clarify one thing, and that was on this tape recorder commands and high and low bit rate.
And we just want to make it clear that preburn the 6-minute callout is High Bit Rate, Record, Forward, Command Reset.
Did you - did you tell us a few minutes ago that you did indeed do all those at about 6 minutes?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, Gordy, I certainly thought I did, but I can't specifically - I know the High Bit Rate was there, because we switched to Low later.
I can't - I'm afraid I can't specifically verify the Command Reset.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gordy, I can specifically say that when those - after the bus stars came on, we called that out.
I'm almost sure Jack did get it, because I had him then check the helium valves and the nitrogen - helium and the nitrogen valves.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We're just trying to make sure if we - or to determine if we do have a switch problem.
The INCO did send Low Bit Rate, Command, just prior to - to LOS there before LOI, which is the way it's called out in the Flight Plan.

And then you should have come along later with a Command Reset, setting the switch to High Bit Rate, and the Command Reset switched into High Bit Rate mode, and for some reason it did not go into High Bit Rate.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, you want to test it out here, before we go around?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's a pretty good thought.
I think we will.
On stand by.
We'll give you - we'll figure out how we're going to do it here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Jack.
We just now commanded Low Bit Rate, and you're in Low Bit Rate.
We'd like you now to select High and Command Reset.
[Pause with pulsing noise on comm link.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
Gordy, you also have an oscillation in uplink signal strength and a clicking in the - in the audio.
Do you read?
We just went steady and at about 60 percent - 70 percent signal strength.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
We hear that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
I'm going to - you want me to go ahead and command High and Command Reset?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's affirmative.
The High Gain just went Wide Beam, but we're still reading you loud and clear.
Go ahead in High Bit Rate and Command Reset.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
High Bit Rate; Command Reset.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The Data Storage Equipment (DSE), also known simply as the tape recorder is now operating.
It's primary function is to record digital sensor information from around the spacecraft that can later be replayed to Earth.
It also includes an analogue voice track that records the voices in the cabin.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay; it seems to have worked properly, Jack.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Gordy.
I may not have got the Command Reset at 6 minutes.
I - I - I just can't tell you right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; I want to assure you it was no big problem as far as the burn goes.
No great loss there.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I hope not.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: America, Houston.
About 5 minutes to LOS now.
We'd like to have you go ahead and get the jet inhibits and the covers open as shown in the Flight Plan so we can see that before we lose you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay; Gordy.
IR Cover is coming Open.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Mark it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay; UV Cover coming Open...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Mark it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And you want me to stay in High Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's affirmative.
You're there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: 090:37:XX Fullerton:You're going to have to do it in a minute anyway, so since you're there already, stay there.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
We're there; we've got tape motion.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, the deadband - or the A/C - B/D, A/C roll has been changed, and you should have the proper jet configuration now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We see it; it looks good, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay; if you're still with us, we're going to Open up the Mapping Camera/Laser Altimeter Cover.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
We're going to Extend the Mapping Camera.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger on that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.
Okay.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Mark.
Barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 090:40:03 -This is Apollo Control.
We're estimating a change of shift briefing at 3:45 Houston time.
One minute and 16 seconds away from Loss of Signal on this first lunar revolution.
Participants in the Flight Director change of shift briefing will be Gerry Griffin who has been in charge of the Gold flight team, Dr.
Royce Hawkins the Flight Surgeon, and Charlie Dumis the EECOM.
At 90 hours, 40 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 090:41:00 -This is Apollo Control at 90 hours, 41 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've had Loss of Signal, as the Apollo 17 spacecraft coasted behind the Moon on the first lunar orbit.
Currently in an orbit measuring 52.6 by 168.6 nautical miles [97.4 by 312.2 km].
Will be up again in about 47 minutes or approximately.
The tables are not up on the display here on the next Acquisition of Signal as to the next time the spacecraft comes around on the second lunar orbit.
Change of shift briefing at 3:45 approximately 10 minutes from now in the small briefing room.
Building 1 news room.
Participants Flight Director Gerry Griffin, Flight Surgeon Dr.
Royce Hawkins, and the EECOM Charlie Dumis.
At 90 hours, 42 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO
